Indeed these aliases are here to bring clarity and meanings in the library, and also to help users (i.e. developers) to know what kind of objects they handle with the same vocabulary as the one used in
, and in general, in the whole design system. They can be seen as a ligh level of abstraction with meanings, without having to define real types with
Type aliases here point to raw tokens aliases, thus by transition they point to real types.
folder is maybe the hotter one.
In fact all the semantic tokens values are defined there, and if a JSON to Swift parser generate files, these files must be stored there. We need any
can embed and override all or some of the tokens, and if a new theme must be implemented from nothing, it must implement all the semantic tokens. Thus each semantic tokens group is declared in a Swft
These protocols list all semantic tokens for each “family”. Thus, any theme implementation (at least
) must implement the values, and if possible through a
so as to keep seperated the objects.
In a nutshell, declare the tokens somewhere in protocols, and define theme in theme implementation.
First, you need to define which from family this semantic comes. We have today 9 families:
If your token is not from one of these groups, maybe you should redesign your token or create a new family. To do that, you will have to create a dedicated Swift
, with a useful name, and declare as a
the semantic token. If you already know the family, just update the matching files.
If you need to define Swift
, update the suitable file in the
folder. If you think users may handle your tokens with tuples, or pack of tokens, you can add your own
Please, respect the nomenclature of the files, e.g. for a new family “Awesome semantic” tokens:
Then, update the unit tests. For each semantic tokens we check if a subtheme can override the token, i.e. update the
by overring the property with a fake value, then compare it to the
containing a default value. If you have defined also composite objects, add unit tests to check if they do their job.

content.text: How to update or remove semantic tokens

inlineContent.text: Quite simple, find the semantic token you want to update or remove, and update or remove it.
But beware, if you change the name of the property or if you move it from a

to another, or if you remove the token, you must keep retrocompatibility as much as possible so as to avoid to break any public API. Keep also the CHANGELOG and/or the release updated with some BREAKING CHANGE notification, and also the Git history clean.
If you update the value, keep also the CHANGELOG and/or RELEASE NOTE updated so as to let yout users know the variables have been changed.
